wiki.summary: "Locked Out of Heaven" is a song by singer-songwriter Bruno Mars released on October 1, 2012 as the lead single to his second album Unorthodox Jukebox. The song was written by Bruno, Philip Lawrence and Ari Levine as The Smeezingtons & produced by Mark Ronson, Jeff Bhasker, Emile Haynie & The Smeezingtons. Locked Out of Heaven is a reggae rock & pop-rock song influenced by new wave & funk, it's lyrics are about the rapturous feelings brought about by a relationship infused with positive emotion as well as euphoria from sex. Read more on Last.fm.
wiki.content: "Locked Out of Heaven" is a song by singer-songwriter Bruno Mars released on October 1, 2012 as the lead single to his second album Unorthodox Jukebox. The song was written by Bruno, Philip Lawrence and Ari Levine as The Smeezingtons & produced by Mark Ronson, Jeff Bhasker, Emile Haynie & The Smeezingtons. Locked Out of Heaven is a reggae rock & pop-rock song influenced by new wave & funk, it's lyrics are about the rapturous feelings brought about by a relationship infused with positive emotion as well as euphoria from sex. Locked Out Of Heaven ranked at #11 on Billboard's Year-End list of 2013 & is currently certified Diamond by the RIAA. Following Bruno's 2010 debut album Doo-Wops & Hooligans, Mars revealed he wanted to create something unexpected with it's follow-up. "This is me going into the studio and recording and writing whatever I want," Bruno said confidently. "This album represents my freedom." It all started backstage, after a show, during a jamming session in a "greenroom", while singing the phrase 'Locked Out of Heaven'. According to Philip Lawrence, the track developed a meaning as they started to write it, "when you’re with someone who's showing you a new way to love." The Smeezingtons went to New York to work with Jeff Bhasker, Emile Haynie and Mark Ronson and during a jamming session with drums, bass, and guitar, Bruno created the riff of the song. He started singing the chorus previously created on the top of the riff. Then, the production team went back to L.A., where they finished the lyrics and changed the original melody. Lawrence felt that the recording was "sort of empty but it had a carnal vibe to it", making them enthusiastic to place a "driving rhythm" in it. He recalled thinking it was "something special" in the studio, the same way he did during the creation of Just the Way You Are & Bruno's feature on Travie McCoy's Billionaire (2010). Bhasker, one of the song's producers, explained the track's roots as it "came in the middle of the process of putting together the album". He elaborated; "We were just having a jam session, tracking some things, and Bruno started playing this groove and making up something on the spot; we all thought it was pretty good. We wound up working a long time on that, trying to get it just right." 'Locked Out of Heaven' began as a "cha-cha-style duet", sounding like Santana's Smooth featuring Rob Thomas (1999), Ronson asked the Dap-Kings to collaborate on the track in order to get a "crisply syncopated, locked-in groove." According to Michael Leonhart, he contributed with "Thriller" vibe horns to the song, however, they didn't make the final version. Regarding the development of the song, the Smeezingtons thought "there's a good pocket on this song right now. Let's keep it going." Bruno also mentioned that it took a long time to create it, commenting: "People didn't see us going at each other's throats in the studio and pulling out our hair." He added, "Trying to get these drums right and figure out a bass line." Lawrence believes the lack of "heavy hitting drums" and the presence of a small guitar solo allows the song to be carried by Bruno. According to Ronson, Bruno wanted Locked Out Of Heaven to be the lead single, which he considered a "brave" choice. Aaron Bay-Schuck, Mars' A&R representative at the time, found the track's production to be cutting edge, although it wasn't an instant favorite. However, he perceived that "it was something special" but had no idea how to classify it. Bay-Schuck felt the song needed time to grow as "the more you hear it, the more of an earworm it becomes", leading the promotional department to make sure that program directors weren't afraid of playing a track that "sounded different from what was on the radio". During his Google Hangout on the day of the song's release, Bruno was asked by a fan to name his favorite lyrics from the track. Bruno picked the phrase "But swimming in your water is something spiritual", & said that the single's exploration of feeling and being in love fits into the "sensual, sensual & sensual" theme of the album. Bruno spoke on The Police's influence on the track, sharing: "Hell yeah! You try to write a Police song!. I grew up listening to The Police, I grew up performing in bars, singing Police songs ... I remember performing a song like 'Roxanne', and you play those first couple of chords, and you hit that first note, and you watch the whole bar ignite. And as an artist, as a songwriter, it's like 'Man, I want to write a song that makes people's eyes explode the first chord!'." Though critics have pointed out the song's similarities to some of the hits by the Police, Bruno told MTV News that he did not intend to write anything mimicking by the Sting-fronted band. Instead, it came to him out of the blue, one night during his studio sessions prior to recording the Unorthodox Jukebox album. "I don't think it initially tried to sound like anybody else, but I picked up the guitar and just started playing [the song's opening chords]," Bruno explained: "That's how it normally works; I'll pick up a guitar and I'll start humming a melody, and I started singing that, and I was up there in Sting-ville, in that register, so that's what you get...". The song's accompanying music video was directed by Cameron Duddy & Bruno and was released on October 15, 2012. The concept of the video shows Bruno having a good time with his bandmates, doing things like smoking, drinking beer and playing games. He is also seen singing the song with his band at a club. The video has a vintage style reminiscent of VHS tapes. The music video was filmed using "an old VHS camera", with Bruno & The Hooligans performing, and playing along with the single live multiple times. This allowed Duddy to record the "feel of a live performance". He added that Bruno "wanted it to feel as if someone's dad filmed it". Bruno said, "The concept is just old-fashioned fun. No story line, it's not me singing to a girl, you get a good sense of what you're going to get live ... It's very VHS-y. I love that man, it takes me back to my childhood, when the tracking is off and the color is off, there's a beauty in that." Cameron Duddy affirmed he gave Mars various "wacky story lines". Nevertheless, Bruno wanted to record a "performance video" as it demonstrated a "hard-rocking image". Locked Out of Heaven debuted at #34 on the Billboard Hot 100 on the week of October 20, 2012, selling 92,000 copies in it's first week. In it's fourth week charting it reached the top 10 for the first time the week of November 10, 2012, rising from #15 to #7 & becoming Bruno's ninth top 10 single. Locked Out Of Heaven soon rose to #2 behind Rihanna's Diamonds & on the week of December 22, 2012 it dethroned "Diamonds" on top of the Hot 100 chart, becoming Bruno's fourth #1 song. This ascension to the top marked the fastest collection of a male artist's first 4 #1's in 48 years, only surpassed by Bobby Vinton. Locked Out of Heaven charted for a second consecutive week atop the Hot 100, with Bruno becoming one of 9 male soloists in the Hot 100's 54-year history to tally at least 2 weeks on top with each of his first 4 leaders. On it's third consecutive week on the top, the song was the first to lead all the 4 tallies (Hot 100, Radio Songs, Digital Songs, On-Demand Songs) simultaneously. Locked Out Of Heaven spent 6 consecutive weeks at the top in December 2012 & January 2013 before it's run was concluded by Macklemore & Ryan Lewis' Thrift Shop featuring Wanz, becoming Bruno's second longest-reigning #1 song behind his feature on Mark Ronson's Uptown Funk with 14 weeks spent at the top aswell as his longest running #1 song as a lead artist.
